{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01JzPCJx6JGeuTp8VCcb68uR","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"This is the key file. Let me understand the block inheritance mechanism:\n\n1. **BlockContext** (lines 14-39):\n   - Uses a `defaultdict(list)` to store blocks - each block name maps to a FIFO queue (list)\n   - `add_blocks()` inserts blocks at position 0 (beginning)\n   - `pop()` removes and returns the last item\n   - `push()` appends to the end\n   - `get_block()` returns the last item without removing it\n\n2. **BlockNode** (lines 42-83):\n   - Has a `name` and `nodelist`\n   - In `render()`:\n     - If no block_context, it renders its own nodelist\n     - If block_context exists, it pops the block with the same name\n     - If nothing popped, it uses itself\n     - Creates a new BlockNode instance to hold context\n     - Renders the block's nodelist\n     - If something was popped, it pushes it back\n\n3. **ExtendsNode** (lines 86-160):\n   - Collects all BlockNodes from its nodelist (children blocks)\n   - In `render()`:\n     - Gets the parent template\n     - Creates a BlockContext if needed\n     - Calls `add_blocks()` with the child blocks\n     - Checks if parent has its own ExtendsNode, and if not (meaning it's root), adds parent's blocks\n     - Renders the parent template\n\nThe issue seems to be in how blocks are added to the context in the ExtendsNode. Let me trace through what happens with 3-level inheritance:\n\ninheritance01: `\"1{% block first %}&{% endblock %}3{% block second %}_{% endblock %}\"`\ninheritance15: extends inheritance01, has `{% block first %}2{% block inner %}inner{% endblock %}{% endblock %}`\ninheritance16: extends inheritance15, has `{% block inner %}out{% endblock %}`\n\n\nWhen rendering inheritance16, the ExtendsNode collects its blocks (`{\"inner\": <BlockNode>}`) and creates a BlockContext to add them. It then retrieves inheritance15 as the parent. Since inheritance15 is also an ExtendsNode, the code at line 149 doesn't add the parent's blocks to the context—it only adds them if the parent is NOT an ExtendsNode. This means blocks from intermediate templates in the chain aren't being properly registered in the context before rendering the parent. This approach prevents duplicate block registration—if the parent template also extends another template, its ExtendsNode will handle propagating blocks up the chain when rendered. Let me trace through inheritance16 more carefully: when rendering inheritance16, the ExtendsNode sets its own blocks in context, then retrieves the compiled parent (inheritance15). Since inheritance15's first non-text node is an ExtendsNode, the code skips adding inheritance15's blocks and breaks. Then calling the parent's render method triggers inheritance15's own ExtendsNode, which continues the chain upward.\n\nThe key insight is that each template's ExtendsNode only collects BlockNodes directly in its nodelist—nested blocks inside other blocks don't get picked up at the root level. So inheritance15's blocks dictionary contains only \"first\" and \"second\" at the top level, while the \"inner\" block nested within \"first\" stays encapsulated. When inheritance16's ExtendsNode runs, it only registers its own \"inner\" block and passes control to the parent template's rendering logic.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":10,"cache_creation_input_tokens":5030,"cache_read_input_tokens":46413,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":5030},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"976e6f04-7fdf-4cc8-9b2b-81bd35751556","uuid":"fd60cc02-7748-46df-9ab7-84ffedfc2b82"}

RemovedInDjango70Warning(PendingDeprecationWarning):\\n27:     pass\\n28: \\n29: \\n30: RemovedAfterNextVersionWarning = RemovedInDjango70Warning\\n31: \\n32: \\n33: class warn_about_renamed_method:\\n34:     def __init__(\\n35:         self, class_name, old_method_name, new_method_name, deprecation_warning\\n36:     ):\\n37:         self.class_name = class_name\\n38:         self.old_method_name = old_method_name\\n39:         self.new_method_name = new_method_name\\n40:         self.deprecation_warning = deprecation_warning\\n41: \\n42:     def __call__(self, f):\\n43:         def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):\\n44:             warnings.warn(\\n45:                 \\\"`%s.%s` is deprecated, use `%s` instead.\\\"\\n46:                 % (self.class_name, self.old_method_name, self.new_method_name),\\n47:                 self.deprecation_warning,\\n48:                 2,\\n49:             )\\n50:             return f(*args, **kwargs)\\n51: \\n52:         return wrapper\\n53: \\n54: \\n55: class RenameMethodsBase(type):\\n56:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n57:     Handles the deprecation paths when renaming a method.\\n58: \\n59:     It does the following:\\n60:         1) Define the new method if missing and complain about it.\\n61:         2) Define the old method if missing.\\n62:         3) Complain whenever an old method is called.\\n63: \\n64:     See #15363 for more details.\\n65:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n66: \\n67:     renamed_methods = ()\\n68: \\n69:     def __new__(cls, name, bases, attrs):\\n70:         new_class = super().__new__(cls, name, bases, attrs)\\n71: \\n72:         for base in inspect.getmro(new_class):\\n73:             class_name = base.__name__\\n74:             for renamed_method in cls.renamed_methods:\\n75:                 old_method_name = renamed_method[0]\\n76:                 old_method = base.__dict__.get(old_method_name)\\n77:                 new_method_name = renamed_method[1]\\n78:                 new_method = base.__dict__.get(new_method_name)\\n79:                 deprecation_warning = renamed_method[2]\\n80:                 wrapper = warn_about_renamed_method(class_name, *renamed_method)\\n81: \\n82:                 # Define the new method if missing and complain about it\\n83:                 if not new_method and old_method:\\n84:                     warnings.warn(\\n85:                         \\\"`%s.%s` method should be renamed `%s`.\\\"\\n86:                         % (class_name, old_method_name, new_method_name),\\n87:                         deprecation_warning,\\n88:                         2,\\n89:                     )\\n90:                     setattr(base, new_method_name, old_method)\\n91:                     setattr(base, old_method_name, wrapper(old_method))\\n92: \\n93:                 # Define the old method as a wrapped call to the new method.\\n94:                 if not old_method and new_method:\\n95:                     setattr(base, old_method_name, wrapper(new_method))\\n96: \\n97:         return new_class\\n98: \\n99: \\n100: def deprecate_posargs(deprecation_warning, remappable_names, /):\\n101:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n102:     Function/method decorator to deprecate some or all positional arguments.\\n103: \\n104:     The decorated function will map any positional arguments after the ``*`` to\\n105:     the corresponding keyword arguments and issue a deprecation warning.\\n106: \\n107:     The decorator takes two arguments: a RemovedInDjangoXXWarning warning\\n108:     category and a list of parameter names that have been changed from\\n109:     positional-or-keyword to keyword-only, in their original positional order.\\n110: \\n111:     Works on both functions and methods. To apply to a class constructor,\\n112:     decorate its __init__() method. To apply to a staticmethod or classmethod,\\n113:     use @deprecate_posargs after @staticmethod or @classmethod.\\n114: \\n115:     Example: to deprecate passing option1 or option2 as posargs, change::\\n116: \\n117:         def some_func(request, option1, option2=True):\\n118:             ...\\n119: \\n120:     to::\\n121: \\n122:         @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\\\"option1\\\", \\\"option2\\\"])\\n123:         def some_func(request, *, option1, option2=True):\\n124:             ...\\n125: \\n126:     After the deprecation period, remove the decorator (but keep the ``*``)::\\n127: \\n128:         def some_func(request, *, option1, option2=True):\\n129:             ...\\n130: \\n131:     Caution: during the deprecation period, do not add any new *positional*\\n132:     parameters or change the remaining ones. For example, this attempt to add a\\n133:     new param would break code using the deprecated posargs::\\n134: \\n135:         @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\\\"option1\\\", \\\"option2\\\"])\\n136:         def some_func(request, wrong_new_param=None, *, option1, option2=True):\\n137:             # Broken: existing code may pass a value intended as option1 in the\\n138:             # wrong_new_param position.\\n139:             ...\\n140: \\n141:     However, it's acceptable to add new *keyword-only* parameters and to\\n142:     re-order the existing ones, so long as the list passed to\\n143:     @deprecate_posargs is kept in the original posargs order. This change will\\n144:     work without breaking existing code::\\n145: \\n146:         @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\\\"option1\\\", \\\"option2\\\"])\\n147:         def some_func(request, *, new_param=None, option2=True, option1):\\n148:             ...\\n149: \\n150:     The @deprecate_posargs decorator adds a small amount of overhead. In most\\n151:     cases it won't be significant, but use with care in performance-critical\\n152:     code paths.\\n153:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n154: \\n155:     def decorator(func):\\n156:         if isinstance(func, type):\\n157:             raise TypeError(\\n158:                 \\\"@deprecate_posargs cannot be applied to a class. (Apply it \\\"\\n159:                 \\\"to the __init__ method.)\\\"\\n160:             )\\n161:         if isinstance(func, classmethod):\\n162:             raise TypeError(\\\"Apply @classmethod before @deprecate_posargs.\\\")\\n163:         if isinstance(func, staticmethod):\\n164:             raise TypeError(\\\"Apply @staticmethod before @deprecate_posargs.\\\")\\n165: \\n166:         params = inspect.signature(func).parameters\\n167:         num_by_kind = Counter(param.kind for param in params.values())\\n168: \\n169:         if num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.VAR_POSITIONAL] > 0:\\n170:             raise TypeError(\\n171:                 \\\"@deprecate_posargs() cannot be used with variable positional `*args`.\\\"\\n172:             )\\n173: \\n174:         num_positional_params = (\\n175:             num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.POSITIONAL_ONLY]\\n176:             + num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD]\\n177:         )\\n178:         num_keyword_only_params = num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.KEYWORD_ONLY]\\n179:         if num_keyword_only_params < 1:\\n180:             raise TypeError(\\n181:                 \\\"@deprecate_posargs() requires at least one keyword-only parameter \\\"\\n182:                 \\\"(after a `*` entry in the parameters list).\\\"\\n183:             )\\n184:         if any(\\n185:             name not in params or params[name].kind != inspect.Parameter.KEYWORD_ONLY\\n186:             for name in remappable_names\\n187:         ):\\n188:             raise TypeError(\\n189:                 \\\"@deprecate_posargs() requires all remappable_names to be \\\"\\n190:                 \\\"keyword-only parameters.\\\"\\n191:             )\\n192: \\n193:         num_remappable_args = len(remappable_names)\\n194:         max_positional_args = num_positional_params + num_remappable_args\\n195: \\n196:         func_name = func.__name__\\n197:         if func_name == \\\"__init__\\\":\\n198:             # In the warning, show \\\"ClassName()\\\" instead of \\\"__init__()\\\".\\n199:             # The class isn't defined yet, but its name is in __qualname__.\\n200:             # Some examples of __qualname__:\\n201:             # - ClassName.__init__\\n202:             # - Nested.ClassName.__init__\\n203:             # - MyTests.test_case.<locals>.ClassName.__init__\\n204:             local_name = func.__qualname__.rsplit(\\\"<locals>.\\\", 1)[-1]\\n205:             class_name = local_name.replace(\\\".__init__\\\", \\\"\\\")\\n206:             func_name = class_name\\n207: \\n208:         def remap_deprecated_args(args, kwargs):\\n209:             \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n210:             Move deprecated positional args to kwargs and issue a warning.\\n211:             Return updated (args, kwargs).\\n212:             \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n213:             if (num_positional_args := len(args)) > max_positional_args:\\n214:                 raise TypeError(\\n215:                     f\\\"{func_name}() takes at most {max_positional_args} positional \\\"\\n216:                     f\\\"argument(s) (including {num_remappable_args} deprecated) but \\\"\\n217:                     f\\\"{num_positional_args} were given.\\\"\\n218:                 )\\n219: \\n220:             #         1) Define the new method if missing and complain about it.\\n61:         2) Define the old method if missing.\\n62:         3) Complain whenever an old method is called.\\n63: \\n64:     See decorator to deprecate some or all positional arguments.\\n103: \\n104:     The decorated function will map any positional arguments after the ``*`` to\\n105:     the corresponding keyword arguments and issue a deprecation warning.\\n106: \\n107:     The decorator takes two arguments: a RemovedInDjangoXXWarning warning\\n108:     category and a list of parameter names that have been changed from\\n109:     positional-or-keyword to keyword-only, in their original positional order.\\n110: \\n111:     Works on both functions and methods. To apply to a class constructor,\\n112:     decorate its __init__() method. To apply to a staticmethod or classmethod,\\n113:     use @deprecate_posargs after @staticmethod or @classmethod.\\n114: \\n115:     Example: to deprecate passing option1 or option2 as posargs, change::\\n116: \\n117:         def some_func(request, option1, option2=True):\\n118:             ...\\n119: \\n120:     to::\\n121: \\n122:         @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\\\"option1\\\", \\\"option2\\\"])\\n123:         def some_func(request, *, option1, option2=True):\\n124:             ...\\n125: \\n126:     After the deprecation period, remove the decorator (but keep the ``*``)::\\n127: \\n128:         def some_func(request, *, option1, option2=True):\\n129:             ...\\n130: \\n131:     Caution: during the deprecation period, do not add any new *positional*\\n132:     parameters or change the remaining ones. For example, this attempt to add a\\n133:     new param would break code using the deprecated posargs::\\n134: \\n135:         @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\\\"option1\\\", \\\"option2\\\"])\\n136:         def some_func(request, wrong_new_param=None, *, option1, option2=True):\\n137:             # Broken: existing code may pass a value intended as option1 in the\\n138:             # wrong_new_param position.\\n139:             ...\\n140: \\n141:     However, it's acceptable to add new *keyword-only* parameters and to\\n142:     re-order the existing ones, so long as the list passed to\\n143:     @deprecate_posargs is kept in the original posargs order. This change will\\n144:     work without breaking existing code::\\n145: \\n146:         @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\\\"option1\\\", \\\"option2\\\"])\\n147:         def some_func(request, *, new_param=None, option2=True, option1):\\n148:             ...\\n149: \\n150:     The @deprecate_posargs decorator adds a small amount of overhead. In most\\n151:     cases it won't be significant, but use with care in performance-critical\\n152:     code paths.\\n153:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n154: \\n155:     def decorator(func):\\n156:         if isinstance(func, type):\\n157:             raise TypeError(\\n158:                 \\\"@deprecate_posargs cannot be applied to a class. will map any positional arguments after the ``*`` to\n105:     the corresponding keyword arguments and issue a deprecation warning.\n106: \n107:     The decorator takes two arguments: a To apply to a class constructor,\n112:     decorate its __init__() method. To apply to a staticmethod or classmethod,\n113:     use @deprecate_posargs after @staticmethod or @classmethod.\n114: \n115:     Example: to deprecate passing option1 or option2 as posargs, change::\n116: \n117:         def some_func(request, option1, option2=True):\n118:             ...\n119: \n120:     to::\n121: \n122:         @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\"option1\", \"option2\"])\n123:         def some_func(request, *, option1, option2=True):\n124:             ...\n125: \n126:     After the deprecation period, remove the decorator (but keep the ``*``)::\n127: \n128:         def some_func(request, *, option1, option2=True):\n129:             ...\n130: \n131:     Caution: during the deprecation period, do not add any new *positional*\n132:     parameters or change the remaining ones. For example, this attempt to add a\n133:     new param would break code using the deprecated posargs::\n134: \n135:         @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\"option1\", \"option2\"])\n136:         def some_func(request, wrong_new_param=None, *, option1, option2=True):\n137:             # Broken: existing code may pass a value intended as option1 in the\n138:             # wrong_new_param position.\n139:             ...\n140: \n141:     However, it's acceptable to add new *keyword-only* parameters and to\n142:     re-order the existing ones, so long as the list passed to\n143:     @deprecate_posargs is kept in the original posargs order. This change will\n144:     work without breaking existing code::\n145: \n146:         @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\"option1\", \"option2\"])\n147:         def some_func(request, *, new_param=None, option2=True, option1):\n148:             ...\n149: \n150:     The @deprecate_posargs decorator adds a small amount of overhead. In most\n151:     cases it won't be significant, but use with care in performance-critical\n152:     code paths.\n153:     \"\"\"\n154: \n155:     def decorator(func):\n156:         if isinstance(func, type):\n157:             raise TypeError(\n158:                 \"@deprecate_posargs cannot be applied to a class. (Apply it \"\n159:                 \"to the __init__ method.)\"\n160:             )\n161:         if isinstance(func, classmethod):\n162:             raise TypeError(\"Apply @classmethod before @deprecate_posargs.\")\n163:         if isinstance(func, staticmethod):\n164:             raise TypeError(\"Apply @staticmethod before @deprecate_posargs.\")\n165: \n166:         params = inspect.signature(func).parameters\n167:         num_by_kind = Counter(param.kind for param in params.values())\n168: \n169:         if num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.VAR_POSITIONAL] > 0:\n170:             raise TypeError(\n171:                 \"@deprecate_posargs() cannot be used with variable positional `*args`.\"\n172:             )\n173: \n174:         num_positional_params = (\n175:             num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.POSITIONAL_ONLY]\n176:             + num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD]\n177:         )\n178:         num_keyword_only_params = num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.KEYWORD_ONLY]\n179:         if num_keyword_only_params < 1:\n180:             raise TypeError(\n181:                 \"@deprecate_posargs() requires at least one keyword-only parameter \"\n182:                 \"(after a `*` entry in the parameters list).\"\n183:             )\n184:         if any(\n185:             name not in params or params[name].kind != inspect.Parameter.KEYWORD_ONLY\n186:             for name in remappable_names\n187:         ):\n188:             raise TypeError(\n189:                 \"@deprecate_posargs() requires all remappable_names to be \"\n190:                 \"keyword-only parameters.\"\n191:             )\n192: \n193:         num_remappable_args = len(remappable_names)\n194:         max_positional_args = num_positional_params + num_remappable_args\n195: \n196:         func_name = func.__name__\n197:         if func_name == \"__init__\":\n198:             # In the warning, show \"ClassName()\" instead of \"__init__()\".\n199:             # The class isn't defined yet, but its name is in __qualname__.\n200:             # Some examples of __qualname__:\n201:             # - ClassName.__init__\n202:             # - Nested.ClassName.__init__\n203:             # - MyTests.test_case.<locals>.ClassName.__init__\n204:             local_name = func.__qualname__.rsplit(\"<locals>.\", 1)[-1]\n205:             class_name = local_name.replace(\".__init__\", \"\")\n206:             func_name = class_name\n207: \n208:         def remap_deprecated_args(args, kwargs):\n209:             \"\"\"\n210:             Move deprecated positional args to kwargs and issue a warning.\n211:             Return updated (args, kwargs).\n212:             \"\"\"\n213:             if (num_positional_args := len(args)) > max_positional_args:\n214:                 raise TypeError(\n215:                     f\"{func_name}() takes at most {max_positional_args} positional \"\n216:                     f\"argument(s) (including {num_remappable_args} deprecated) but \"\n217:                     f\"{num_positional_args} were given.\"\n218:                 )\n219: \n220:             # Identify which of the _potentially remappable_ params are\n221:             # actually _being remapped_ in this particular call.\n222:             remapped_names = remappable_names[\n223:                 : num_positional_args - num_positional_params\n224:             ]\n225:             conflicts = set(remapped_names) & set(kwargs)\n226:             if conflicts:\n227:                 # Report duplicate names in the original parameter order.\n228:                 conflicts_str = \", \".join(\n229:                     f\"'{name}'\" for name in remapped_names if name in conflicts\n230:                 )\n231:                 raise TypeError(\n232:                     f\"{func_name}() got both deprecated positional and keyword \"\n233:                     f\"argument values for {conflicts_str}.\"\n234:                 )\n235: \n236:             # Do the remapping.\n237:             remapped_kwargs = dict(\n238:                 zip(remapped_names, args[num_positional_params:], strict=True)\n239:             )\n240:             remaining_args = args[:num_positional_params]\n241:             updated_kwargs = kwargs | remapped_kwargs\n242: \n243:             # Issue the deprecation warning.\n244:             remapped_names_str = \", \".join(f\"'{name}'\" for name in remapped_names)\n245:             warnings.warn(\n246:                 f\"Passing positional argument(s) {remapped_names_str} to {func_name}() \"\n247:                 \"is deprecated.  1) Define the new method if missing and complain about it.\n        2) Define the old method if missing.\n        3) Complain whenever an old method is called.\n\n    See #15363 for more details.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    renamed_methods = ()\n\n    def __new__(cls, name, bases, attrs):\n        new_class = super().__new__(cls, name, bases, attrs)\n\n        for base in inspect.getmro(new_class):\n            class_name = base.__name__\n            for renamed_method in cls.renamed_methods:\n                old_method_name = renamed_method[0]\n                old_method = base.__dict__.get(old_method_name)\n                new_method_name = renamed_method[1]\n                new_method = base.__dict__.get(new_method_name)\n                deprecation_warning =    setattr(base, old_method_name, wrapper(new_method))\n\n        return new_class\n\n\ndef deprecate_posargs(deprecation_warning, remappable_names, /):\n    \"\"\"\n    Function/method decorator to deprecate some or all positional arguments.\n\n    The decorated function will map any positional arguments after the ``*`` to\n    the corresponding keyword arguments and issue a deprecation warning.\n\n    The decorator takes two arguments: a RemovedInDjangoXXWarning warning\n    category and a list of parameter names that have been changed from\n    positional-or-keyword to keyword-only, in their original positional order.\n\n    Works on both functions and methods. To apply to a class constructor,\n    decorate its __init__() method. To apply to a staticmethod or classmethod,\n    use @deprecate_posargs after @staticmethod or @classmethod.\n\n    Example: to deprecate passing option1 or option2 as posargs, change::\n\n        def some_func(request, option1, option2=True):\n            ...\n\n    to::\n\n        @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\"option1\", \"option2\"])\n        def some_func(request, *, option1, option2=True):\n            ...\n\n    After the deprecation period, remove the decorator (but keep the ``*``)::\n\n        def some_func(request, *, option1, option2=True):\n            ...\n\n    Caution: during the deprecation period, do not add any new *positional*\n    parameters or change the remaining ones. For example, this attempt to add a\n    new param would break code using the deprecated posargs::\n\n        @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\"option1\", \"option2\"])\n        def some_func(request, wrong_new_param=None, *, option1, option2=True):\n            # Broken: existing code may pass a value intended as option1 in the\n            # wrong_new_param position.\n            ...\n\n    However, it's acceptable to add new *keyword-only* parameters and to\n    re-order the existing ones, so long as the list passed to\n    @deprecate_posargs is kept in the original posargs order. This change will\n    work without breaking existing code::\n\n        @deprecate_posargs(RemovedInDjangoXXWarning, [\"option1\", \"option2\"])\n        def some_func(request, *, new_param=None, option2=True, option1):\n            ...\n\n    The @deprecate_posargs decorator adds a small amount of overhead. In most\n    cases it won't be significant, but use with care in performance-critical\n    code paths.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    def decorator(func):\n        if isinstance(func, type):\n            raise TypeError(\n                \"@deprecate_posargs cannot be applied to a class. (Apply it \"\n                \"to the __init__ method.)\"\n            )\n        if isinstance(func, classmethod):\n            raise TypeError(\"Apply @classmethod before @deprecate_posargs.\")\n        if isinstance(func, staticmethod):\n            raise TypeError(\"Apply @staticmethod before @deprecate_posargs.\")\n\n        params = inspect.signature(func).parameters\n        num_by_kind = Counter(param.kind for param in params.values())\n\n        if num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.VAR_POSITIONAL] > 0:\n            raise TypeError(\n                \"@deprecate_posargs() cannot be used with variable positional `*args`.\"\n            )\n\n        num_positional_params = (\n            num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.POSITIONAL_ONLY]\n            + num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.POSITIONAL_OR_KEYWORD]\n        )\n        num_keyword_only_params = num_by_kind[inspect.Parameter.KEYWORD_ONLY]\n        if num_keyword_only_params < 1:\n            raise TypeError(\n                \"@deprecate_posargs() requires at least one keyword-only parameter \"\n                \"(after a `*` entry in the parameters list).\"\n            )\n        if any(\n            name not in params or params[name].kind != inspect.Parameter.KEYWORD_ONLY\n            for name in remappable_names\n        ):\n            raise TypeError(\n                \"@deprecate_posargs() requires all remappable_names to be \"\n                \"keyword-only parameters.\"\n            )\n\n        num_remappable_args = len(remappable_names)\n        max_positional_args = num_positional_params + num_remappable_args\n\n        func_name = func.__name__\n        if func_name == \"__init__\":\n            # In the warning, show \"ClassName()\" instead of \"__init__()\".\n            # The class isn't defined yet, but its name is in __qualname__.\n            # Some examples of __qualname__:\n            # - ClassName.__init__\n            # - Nested.ClassName.__init__\n            # - MyTests.test_case.<locals>.ClassName.__init__\n            local_name = func.__qualname__.rsplit(\"<locals>.\", 1)[-1]\n            class_name = local_name.replace(\".__init__\", \"\")\n            func_name = class_name\n\n        def remap_deprecated_args(args, kwargs):\n            \"\"\"\n            Move deprecated positional args to kwargs and issue a warning.\n            Return updated (args, kwargs).\n            \"\"\"\n            if (num_positional_args := len(args)) > max_positional_args:\n                raise TypeError(\n                    f\"{func_name}() takes at most {max_positional_args} positional \"\n                    f\"argument(s) (including {num_remappable_args} deprecated) but \"\n                    f\"{num_positional_args} were given.\"\n                )\n\n            # Identify which of the _potentially remappable_ params are\n            # actually _being remapped_ in this particular call.\n            remapped_names = remappable_names[\n                : num_positional_args - num_positional_params\n            ]\n            conflicts = set(remapped_names) & set(kwargs)\n            if conflicts:\n                # Report duplicate names in the original parameter order.\n                conflicts_str = \", \".join(\n                    f\"'{name}'\" for name in remapped_names if name in conflicts\n                )\n                raise TypeError(\n                    f\"{func_name}() got both deprecated positional and keyword \"\n                    f\"argument values for {conflicts_str}.\"\n                )\n\n            # Do the remapping.\n            remapped_kwargs = dict(\n                zip(remapped_names, args[num_positional_params:], strict=True)\n            )\n            remaining_args = args[:num_positional_params]\n            updated_kwargs = kwargs | remapped_kwargs\n\n            # Issue the deprecation warning.\n            remapped_names_str = \", \".join(f\"'{name}'\" for name in remapped_names)\n            warnings.warn(\n                f\"Passing positional argument(s) {remapped_names_str} to {func_name}() \"\n                \"is deprecated. Use keyword arguments instead.\",\n                deprecation_warning,\n                skip_file_prefixes=django_file_prefixes(),\n            )\n\n            return remaining_args, updated_kwargs\n\n        if iscoroutinefunction(func):\n\n            @functools.wraps(func)\n            async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):\n                if len(args) > num_positional_params:\n                    args, kwargs = remap_deprecated_args(args, kwargs)\n                return await func(*args, **kwargs)\n\n        else:\n\n            @functools.wraps(func)\n            def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):\n                if len(args) > num_positional_params:\n                    args, kwargs = remap_deprecated_args(args, kwargs)\n                return func(*args, **kwargs)\n\n        return wrapper\n\n    return decorator\n\n\nclass MiddlewareMixin:\n    sync_capable = True\n    async_capable = True\n\n    def __init__(self, get_response):\n        if get_response is None:\n            raise ValueError(\"get_response must be provided.\")\n        self.get_response = get_response\n        # If get_response is a coroutine function, turns us into async mode so\n        # a thread is not consumed during a whole request.\n        self.async_mode = iscoroutinefunction(self.get_response)\n        if self.async_mode:\n            # Mark the class as async-capable, but do the actual switch inside\n            # __call__ to avoid swapping out dunder methods.\n            markcoroutinefunction(self)\n        super().__init__()\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return \"<%s get_response=%s>\" % (\n            self.__class__.__qualname__,\n            getattr(\n                self.get_response,\n                \"__qualname__\",\n                self.get_response.__class__.__name__,\n            ),\n        )\n\n    def __call__(self, request):\n        # Exit out to async mode, if needed\n        if self.async_mode:\n            return self.__acall__(request)\n        response = None\n        if hasattr(self, \"process_request\"):\n            response = self.process_request(request)\n        response = response or self.get_response(request)\n        if hasattr(self, \"process_response\"):\n            response = self.process_response(request, response)\n        return response\n\n    async def __acall__(self, request):\n        \"\"\"\n        Async version of __call__ that is swapped in when an async request\n        is running.\n        \"\"\"\n        response = None\n        if hasattr(self, \"process_request\"):\n            response = await sync_to_async(\n                self.process_request,\n                thread_sensitive=True,\n            )(request)\n        response = response or await self.get_response(request)\n        if hasattr(self, \"process_response\"):\n            response = await sync_to_async(\n                self.process_response,\n                thread_sensitive=True,\n            )(request, response)\n        return response\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":3,"oldLines":8,"newStart":3,"newLines":15,"lines":[" import os"," import warnings"," from collections import Counter","-from inspect import iscoroutinefunction, markcoroutinefunction","+from inspect import iscoroutinefunction"," ","+try:","+    from inspect import markcoroutinefunction","+except ImportError:","+    # {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01FM8xcWeYqi19c6fEMyY2nt","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"So there are no uncommitted changes in loader_tags.py. The issue description says \"the regression was introduced sometime recently\", but maybe it's actually testing a specific scenario that I haven't created yet.\n\nLet me look more carefully at what could cause the bug. Looking at the ExtendsNode.render() code again:\n\n```python\ndef render(self, context):\n    compiled_parent = self.get_parent(context)\n\n    if BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY not in context.render_context:\n        context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY] = BlockContext()\n    block_context = context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY]\n\n    # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\n    block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n\n    # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\n    # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n    for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n        # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\n        if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n            if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n                blocks = {\n                    n.name: n\n                    for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n                }\n                block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n            break\n\n    # Call Template._render explicitly so the parser context stays\n    # the same.\n    with context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):\n        return compiled_parent._render(context)\n```\n\nThe issue is that add_blocks is inserting blocks at position 0 every time. So if we have 3 levels:\n\nLevel 1 (inheritance01): blocks = {\"first\": B1, \"second\": S1}\nLevel 2 (inheritance15): blocks = {\"first\": B2, \"inner\": I2}\n\n\nLevel 3 (inheritance16): blocks = {\"inner\": I3}\n\nWhen rendering inheritance16:\n1. ExtendsNode16.render():\n   - add_blocks({\"inner\": I3})\n   - context.blocks[\"inner\"] = [I3]\n   - parent is inheritance15 which has ExtendsNode, so don't add blocks\n   - call inheritance15._render()\n\n2. ExtendsNode15.render():\n   - add_blocks({\"first\": B2, \"inner\": I2})\n   - context.blocks[\"first\"] = [B2]\n   - context.blocks[\"inner\"] = [I2, I3]\n   - parent is inheritance01 which doesn't have ExtendsNode, so add its blocks too\n   - context.blocks[\"first\"] = [B1, B2]\n   - context.blocks[\"second\"] = [S1]\n   - context.blocks[\"inner\"] = [I2, I3]\n   - call inheritance01._render()\n\nWhen popping \"inner\", I3 comes out last, which is correct. But I need to verify the add_blocks implementation—it inserts at position 0, so blocks get prepended rather than appended. After three add operations, the order would be reversed from what I initially thought.\n\nAfter add_blocks({\"inner\": I3}), blocks[\"inner\"] = [I3]. Then add_blocks({\"first\": B2, \"inner\": I2}) gives blocks[\"first\"] = [B2] and blocks[\"inner\"] = [I2, I3]. Finally, add_blocks({\"first\": B1, \"second\": S1}) results in blocks[\"first\"] = [B1, B2], blocks[\"inner\"] = [I2, I3], and blocks[\"second\"] = [S1]. The innermost blocks end up at the end of each list, so pop() retrieves them correctly. But there might be something else—perhaps the middle template's blocks are being added in a way that affects the resolution order.\n\nLooking back at the error message about content from the middle template leaking through suggests the issue is that the middle template's content is showing up when it shouldn't. I should test a case where the middle template doesn't override a block that the innermost one does. Actually, I'm noticing something important: in the test case, inheritance15 defines a structure where BlockNode(\"inner\") is nested inside BlockNode(\"first\")'s nodelist. When rendering BlockNode(\"first\"), that nested BlockNode(\"inner\") gets rendered as part of the nodelist. But this nested block isn't a separate top-level block—it's contained within another block. So when retrieving blocks, there's a distinction between top-level blocks and blocks nested inside other blocks' content.\n\nThe rendering flow needs careful examination. When inheritance01 renders and encounters BlockNode(\"first\"), it calls BlockNode.render() with that name. The block_context.pop(\"first\") should return the correct override from the inheritance chain. After all the adds, the list is [B1, B2], so pop() returns B2 as expected. The question is whether nested blocks within that nodelist are being rendered with the correct context.\n\nWhen B2's nodelist is rendered (containing \"2{% block inner %}inner{% endblock %}\"), it encounters BlockNode(\"inner\"). At this point, block_context.pop(\"inner\") needs to return the right override from [I2, I3]. The concern is whether the pop and push logic properly maintains separate stacks for different block names—popping \"first\" shouldn't affect the \"inner\" stack. Looking at the render method, it uses block_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY) to retrieve the context, then manages the stacks for each named block independently. Each block name maintains its own stack, so popping and pushing happens independently for each. When rendering \"first\", we pop B2 from its stack, render it (which includes the nested \"inner\" block), then restore B2. The \"inner\" block follows the same pattern—popping I3, rendering its content, then pushing it back. This nested structure should execute correctly, so the issue must lie elsewhere.\n\nThe problem might be that BlockNode(\"inner\") is referencing the wrong instance. When parent blocks are added to the stack, we're pulling BlockNodes from the parent's nodelist, but if the parent contains nested blocks, we could be adding incorrect references. Tracing through the actual blocks: inheritance15's nodelist has an ExtendsNode and BlockNode(\"first\") containing a nested BlockNode(\"inner\"). When get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode) is called, it returns both the \"first\" block from inheritance15 and the \"inner\" block nested within it—but this \"inner\" block is the one from the parent template, not necessarily the correct one for the current context.\n\nThe real issue might surface when there are multiple inheritance levels involved. With nested blocks across several template layers, the block resolution could break down.\n\nLet me test this against Django's actual test suite to see what fails.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":151,"cache_read_input_tokens":80248,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":151},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"976e6f04-7fdf-4cc8-9b2b-81bd35751556","uuid":"55a7ade2-db2b-4b3d-a9a5-9379dd6a793c"}

Basic 3-level with nested blocks\n                        'base.html': '{% block outer %}BASE_OUTER{% endblock %}',\n                        'middle.html': '{% extends \"base.html\" %}{% block outer %}MIDDLE_OUTER{% block inner %}MIDDLE_INNER{% endblock %}{% endblock %}',\n                        'child.html': '{% extends \"middle.html\" %}{% block inner %}CHILD_INNER{% endblock %}',\n\n                        # 4-level hierarchy\n                        'base2.html': '{% block level1 %}L1{% endblock %}',\n                        'level2.html': '{% extends \"base2.html\" %}{% block level1 %}L1_L2{% block level2inner %}L2I{% endblock %}{% endblock %}',\n                        'level3.html': '{% extends \"level2.html\" %}{% block level2inner %}L2I_L3{% endblock %}',\n                        'level4.html': '{% extends \"level3.html\" %}{% block level2inner %}L2I_L4{% endblock %}',\n\n                        # Complex nested case\n                        'complex_base.html': '{% block a %}A{% block b %}B{% endblock %}{% endblock %}',\n                        'complex_mid1.html': '{% extends \"complex_base.html\" %}{% block a %}A1{% block b %}B1{% endblock %}{% endblock %}',\n                        'complex_mid2.html': '{% extends \"complex_mid1.html\" %}{% block b %}B2{% endblock %}',\n                        'complex_child.html': '{% extends \"complex_mid2.html\" %}{% block b %}B_CHILD{% endblock %}',\n                    }),\n                ],\n            },\n        }],\n        INSTALLED_APPS=[],\n    )\n    django.setup()\n\nfrom django.template import engines\n\n# Get the Django templates backend\nengine = engines['django']\n\ndef test_case(name, template_name, expected):\n    \"\"\"Test a template rendering\"\"\"\n    try:\n        template = engine.get_template(template_name)\n        output = template.render({})\n        status = 'PASS' if output == expected else 'FAIL'\n        print(f\"{name:<50} {'PASS' if status == 'PASS' else 'FAIL':>6}  |  Expected: {expected:>20}  |  Got: {output:>20}\")\n        if status == 'FAIL':\n            return False\n        return True\n    except Exception as e:\n        print(f\"{name:<50} ERROR |  {str(e)}\")\n        import traceback\n        traceback.print_exc()\n        return False\n\nprint(\"=\" * 120)\nprint(\"Testing nested block inheritance (3+ levels)\")\nprint(\"=\" * 120)\nprint()\n\nall_pass = True\n\n# 3-level inheritance with nested blocks\nprint(\"3-level inheritance with nested blocks:\")\nprint(\"-\" * 120)\nall_pass &= test_case(\n    \"child.html (3-level, override inner block)\",\n    \"child.html\",\n    \"MIDDLE_OUTER_child level 2 CHILD_INNER\"  # WAIT - let me think about what this should be\n)\nprint()\n\n# Actually, let me reconsider what the output should be\n# base.html renders: BASE_OUTER\n# middle.html extends base and overrides outer: MIDDLE_OUTER and inner block: MIDDLE_INNER\n# child.html extends middle and overrides inner: CHILD_INNER\n# So the output should be: MIDDLE_OUTER (from middle which overrides base) + CHILD_INNER (from child which overrides the inner block)\n\nall_pass &= test_case(\n    \"child.html (should show middle outer + child inner)\",\n    \"child.html\",\n    \"MIDDLE_OUTERMIDDLE_INNERMIDDLE_OUTER_CHILD_INNER\"  # Hmm, this doesn't seem right either\n)\nprint()\n\n# Let me re-think the expected output\n# base.html: {% block outer %}BASE_OUTER{% endblock %}\n# middle.html: {% extends \"base.html\" %}{% block outer %}MIDDLE_OUTER{% block inner %}MIDDLE_INNER{% endblock %}{% endblock %}\n# So middle.html inherits from base.html, and overrides the \"outer\" block with \"MIDDLE_OUTER...MIDDLE_INNER...\"\n# child.html: {% extends \"middle.html\" %}{% block inner %}CHILD_INNER{% endblock %}\n# child.html inherits from middle.html, and overrides the \"inner\" block (which is nested inside \"outer\") with \"CHILD_INNER\"\n#\n# Expected: MIDDLE_OUTERMIDDLE_OUTER (wait that's duplicated)\n\n# Let me re-parse:\n# base.html: {% block outer %}BASE_OUTER{% endblock %}\n#   outputs as: BASE_OUTER\n#\n# middle.html: {% extends \"base.html\" %}{% block outer %}MIDDLE_OUTER{% block inner %}MIDDLE_INNER{% endblock %}{% endblock %}\n#   extends base, overrides \"outer\" block with: MIDDLE_OUTER[inner block]MIDDLE_OUTER\n#   wait, the block tag structure is: {% block outer %}MIDDLE_OUTER{% block inner %}MIDDLE_INNER{% endblock %}{% endblock %}\n#   so the content is: MIDDLE_OUTER<inner block content>\n#   if inner block has default \"MIDDLE_INNER\", then output is: MIDDLE_OUTERMIDDLE_INNER\n#\n# child.html: {% extends \"middle.html\" %}{% block inner %}CHILD_INNER{% endblock %}\n#   extends middle, overrides \"inner\" block with: CHILD_INNER\n#   so the output should be: MIDDLE_OUTERCCHILD_INNER\n\n# Let me simplify - the expected output for child.html should be:\n# - outer block from middle = \"MIDDLE_OUTER\" + inner block override + nothing after\n# - inner block overridden to \"CHILD_INNER\"\n# So: MIDDLE_OUTERCCHILD_INNER\n\n# Wait, I made an error in my template definition. Let me re-examine:\n# middle.html: '{% extends \"base.html\" %}{% block outer %}MIDDLE_OUTER{% block inner %}MIDDLE_INNER{% endblock %}{% endblock %}'\n# This says: override the \"outer\" block with \"MIDDLE_OUTER\" + [inner block] which defaults to \"MIDDLE_INNER\"\n# So the full content of the overridden \"outer\" block is: \"MIDDLE_OUTER\" + inner block content\n# If inner block defaults to \"MIDDLE_INNER\", then middle.html renders as: MIDDLE_OUTERMIDDLE_INNER\n\n# child.html overrides the inner block to just \"CHILD_INNER\"\n# So child.html should render as: MIDDLE_OUTERCCHILD_INNER\n\n# Hmm, there's a typo - I wrote \"CCHILD\" but it should be \"CHILD\". Let me fix the expected value:\n# Expected for child.html: MIDDLE_OUTERCCHILD_INNER (still has CC which is wrong)\n\n# Let me look at my template definition again:\n# 'middle.html': '{% extends \"base.html\" %}{% block outer %}MIDDLE_OUTER{% block inner %}MIDDLE_INNER{% endblock %}{% endblock %}'\n# The content between {% block outer %} and {% endblock %} is:\n# MIDDLE_OUTER{% block inner %}MIDDLE_INNER{% endblock %}\n# So when rendered with default inner block, it's: MIDDLE_OUTER + MIDDLE_INNER\n# = MIDDLE_OUTERMIDDLE_INNER\n\n# child.html: {% extends \"middle.html\" %}{% block inner %}CHILD_INNER{% endblock %}\n# This overrides the \"inner\" block with \"CHILD_INNER\"\n# So it should render as: MIDDLE_OUTER + CHILD_INNER\n# = MIDDLE_OUTERCCHILD_INNER\n\n# No wait, I keep typing CCHILD. It should be MIDDLE_OUTERCCHILD_INNER... no that's still wrong.\n# Let me be very careful:\n# MIDDLE_OUTER (text) + CHILD_INNER (content of inner block)\n# = MIDDLE_OUTER + CHILD_INNER (concatenated)\n# = MIDDLE_OUTERCCHILD_INNER\n\n# I keep making the same typo! Let me check my template definition one more time...\n# Oh! I see the issue. In my definition, the inner block should be inside the outer block content.\n# Let me rewrite:\n# middle.html content between outer block tags: \"MIDDLE_OUTER{% block inner %}MIDDLE_INNER{% endblock %}\"\n# when rendered: \"MIDDLE_OUTER\" + [result of inner block]\n# The inner block result would be \"MIDDLE_INNER\" by default\n# So: \"MIDDLE_OUTERMIDDLE_INNER\"\n\n# And when child overrides inner to \"CHILD_INNER\", it becomes:\n# \"MIDDLE_OUTERCCHILD_INNER\"\n\n# Wait, that STILL has the CC duplication. Let me trace through character by character:\n# Text: M-I-D-D-L-E-_-O-U-T-E-R\n# Then: C-H-I-L-D-_-I-N-N-E-R\n# Result: M-I-D-D-L-E-_-O-U-T-E-R-C-H-I-L-D-_-I-N-N-E-R\n# = MIDDLE_OUTERCCHILD_INNER\n\n# Oh! {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01EWShe46vmzrjjdDuMzDPtB","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Interesting! The tests are passing. So there must be a specific scenario that triggers the regression. Let me look more carefully at the incident description again. It says:\n\n\"When a template uses three or more levels of inheritance (e.g., `base.html` -> `layout.html` -> `page.html`), and the innermost template overrides a block that is nested inside another overridden block, the content from the middle template \"leaks through\" — meaning the middle template's block content appears instead of the innermost template's override.\"\n\nSo the issue is that the MIDDLE template's content appears, not the INNERMOST template's. Let me create a test case that would show this symptom.\n\nMaybe the issue is that when we're rendering the middle template's overridden blocks, we're not properly handling the block context for nested blocks. Let me think about what could cause the middle template's content to leak through...\n\nActually, I wonder if the issue is related to how the blocks are being added to the context. What if the problem is that when we add blocks from the middle level, we're somehow overwriting or shadowing the blocks from the child level?\n\nLet me re-read the ExtendsNode.render() code one more time to see if there's an issue...\n\nActually, wait. I just realized something. Look at this code:\n\n```python\ndef render(self, context):\n    compiled_parent = self.get_parent(context)    if BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY not in context.render_context:\n        context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY] = BlockContext()    block_context = context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY]\n\n    # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\n    block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n\n    # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,    # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n    for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n        # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.        if not isinstance(node, TextNode):            if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n                blocks = {                    n.name: n                    for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n                }                block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n            break```\n\nI see the issue now. Line 142 adds the current level's blocks, and line 154 adds the parent's blocks if the parent is the root. But if the parent also extends something, then the parent's ExtendsNode will execute and add its own blocks. This means the current level's blocks get added first, then the parent's blocks get added, which could cause the wrong blocks to be used when rendering nested overrides.\n\nThe execution flow should be: child's ExtendsNode adds child blocks, retrieves parent template, skips adding parent blocks if parent extends, calls parent's render, parent's ExtendsNode adds parent blocks, retrieves its parent template, adds base blocks, then calls base's render. This ordering looks right, so the problem might be elsewhere. When the middle template's content \"leaks through,\" it could mean the middle template's default block content is being rendered instead of the child's override. I should test this with a concrete example: base.html contains \"1{% block first %}&{% endblock %}3\" and middle.html extends base with \"{% block first %}M{% block inner %}MI{% endblock %}{% endblock %}\" while child.html extends middle with \"{% block inner %}CI{% endblock %}\". The expected output is \"1MCI3\", but if leakage occurs we'd see \"1MMI3\" instead. My tests produce the correct output though. The regression might be triggered by a more specific edge case—perhaps involving certain block nesting patterns or conditions I haven't tested yet.\n\nI should consider scenarios like multiple blocks sharing the same name across different nesting levels, parent blocks being overridden while containing child blocks, or block.super usage in nested contexts. Looking at the BlockContext implementation, I notice the insert(0, block) operation adds blocks to the beginning of the list, while pop() removes from the end—this could mean we're removing the oldest block rather than the newest, which would be incorrect.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"cache_creation_input_tokens":166,"cache_read_input_tokens":89148,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":166},"output_tokens":2,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"976e6f04-7fdf-4cc8-9b2b-81bd35751556","uuid":"196eeb08-a9a7-4d23-86bc-e17c6c83a459"}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_016wfM1XerzffTjRYNJo3vJX","type":"tool_result","content":"","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"976e6f04-7fdf-4cc8-9b2b-81bd35751556","uuid":"b2447c82-928e-45b8-9996-7ce618a3fd48","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"","stderr":"","interrupted":false,"isImage":false,"returnCodeInterpretation":"No matches found","noOutputExpected":false}}
{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_015CyQDoJdaGm1sAQDCd9m17","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Let me think about this problem more systematically. The incident report says:\n\n\"When a template uses three or more levels of inheritance (e.g., `base.html` -> `layout.html` -> `page.html`), and the innermost template overrides a block that is nested inside another overridden block, the content from the middle template \"leaks through\" — meaning the middle template's block content appears instead of the innermost template's override.\"\n\nLet me break this down:\n1. Template hierarchy: base -> layout -> page\n2. Condition: page overrides a block that is nested inside another overridden block\n3. Symptom: layout's block content appears instead of page's\n\nSo the issue is that the MIDDLE level's block content appears instead of the INNERMOST level's override.\n\nLet me think about how this could happen. If the middle level's block is appearing, that means:\n- We're rendering the middle level's BlockNode instance instead of the child level's BlockNode instance\n- OR we're not properly overriding the block in the block context\n\nWait! I just realized something. When we have nested blocks, and we add blocks to the context, we're adding BY NAME. If both the middle level and child level define blocks with the same name, then we need to make sure the child level's block takes precedence.\n\nBut my tests show that it does work correctly. So maybe the issue is more subtle...\n\nActually, let me re-examine the add_blocks function:\n\n```python\ndef add_blocks(self, blocks):\n    for name, block in blocks.items():\n        self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n```\n\nThis inserts at position 0. So if we already have blocks with that name, the new block goes to the front.But wait, when we call add_blocks multiple times, the ORDER of insertion matters because of the list operations.Let me trace through again very carefully:\n\n1. ExtendsNode16.render() calls add_blocks({\"inner\": B16})\n   - blocks[\"inner\"] = [B16]\n\n\n2. Then it calls compiled_parent._render() which eventually runs ExtendsNode15.render()\n3. ExtendsNode15.render() calls add_blocks({\"first\": B15, \"inner\": B15})\n   - blocks[\"first\"] = [B15]\n   - blocks[\"inner\"] = [B15, B16] (B15 inserted at position 0, pushing B16 to position 1)\n4. Then ExtendsNode15 calls add_blocks({\"first\": B01, \"second\": B01})\n   - blocks[\"first\"] = [B01, B15] (B01 inserted at position 0, B15 moves to position 1)\n   - blocks[\"second\"] = [B01]\n\nThe key insight is that when blocks are added to the context, they're prepended to the list, so the order of precedence depends on when each level's blocks are registered. The innermost template's blocks should be at the front of the list to take priority, but if the middle template's blocks are being added after the innermost ones, they could end up taking precedence instead. The logic for tracking blocks appears sound. Let me trace through the three-level inheritance chain more carefully.\n\nWith inheritance23 extending inheritance20 extending inheritance01, the rendering process should: first add inheritance23's blocks, then inheritance20's blocks, then inheritance01's base blocks. When rendering inheritance01, the \"first\" block should resolve through the chain—inheritance23's version calls `block.super` to get inheritance20's version, which itself calls `block.super` to get inheritance01's base content. The \"second\" block only exists in inheritance01, so it renders as-is. This cascading resolution through `block.super` is what produces the correct output. After rendering B20, it gets pushed back onto the block_context, so the stack becomes [B01, B20] again. Let me trace through the pop/push logic more carefully—when we pop a block, we store it in the `push` variable, render the nodelist, and then push that original block back if it wasn't None. This means the block_context is restored to its previous state after each render.\n\nThe issue might be in how `block.super()` retrieves blocks. It checks if the block exists in render_context and calls `get_block(self.name)`, which looks up the block by name in the blocks dictionary.\n\nWhen `get_block` is called, it returns the last item in the blocks list for that name—the innermost definition closest to the child template. Then `block.super()` calls `self.render(self.context)` to render that block.\n\nThe problem is that `self.render()` will pop from the block context again, potentially causing the same block to be rendered multiple times or in the wrong order. Tracing through the execution: when `BlockNode01(\"first\").render()` is called, it pops \"first\" from the block context, renders its nodelist containing `{{ block.super }}a`, and when `block.super()` is invoked, it retrieves the block and calls render again—but this second render operation will pop the same block context entry, leading to unexpected behavior.\n\nI'm realizing there might be a deeper issue with how `ExtendsNode` collects blocks from its nodelist using `get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)`. This approach retrieves all BlockNodes in the entire subtree, which could be problematic if multiple BlockNodes share the same name but exist at different nesting levels.\n\nConsider a scenario where inheritance15 has nested blocks with the same names as inheritance16 — when we call `get_nodes_by_type()` on inheritance16, we get its own BlockNode instances, not the parent's. The blocks dictionary maps names to these specific instances. This should work correctly since we're getting the right template's nodes, but it's worth verifying the instance matching is happening as expected.\n\nThe real issue might be in how blocks are ordered when added to the list. Currently, the child's blocks are inserted first, then the parent's blocks are prepended at position 0, resulting in `[parent, child]`. When we pop, we get the child, which seems right. But what if reversing this order — adding parent blocks first, then child blocks — would actually fix the problem? Let me trace through what happens if we swap the insertion order.\n\nIf parent blocks were added first, then child blocks added after, the final list would be `[B16, B01, B15]` for the \"first\" block name. Calling pop() would return B15, which is incorrect — we need B16. So the current order is actually correct.\n\nI need to step through the actual code execution with real block names to identify where things go wrong. Looking more carefully at the ExtendsNode.render() method around lines 146-155, there's something about how blocks are being collected from the compiled parent's nodelist that might be the culprit.\n\nThe code iterates through nodes in the parent's nodelist, and when it finds a non-TextNode that isn't an ExtendsNode, it collects all BlockNodes from the parent's nodelist using get_nodes_by_type(). This seems right, but I should verify the logic is actually working as intended.\n\nMaybe the issue manifests when there's default block content at the middle template level that doesn't get overridden by the child — that content could be leaking into the output when it shouldn't. Let me construct a specific test case to check this scenario.\n\nThe actual problem appears to be about three-level inheritance where the innermost template overrides a block nested inside another overridden block, causing the middle template's content to appear instead. For instance: base.html defines an outer block, layout.html overrides it with content containing a nested inner block, and page.html overrides just the inner block. The bug would be layout.html's inner content showing up rather than page.html's override. I've already tested this exact scenario with inheritance15/16 and it passed, so either the issue has been resolved or it exists elsewhere in the code.\n\nI should investigate whether there's a specific git branch or commit where this bug was introduced, or if the problem manifests under slightly different conditions than what I've tested.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":156,"cache_read_input_tokens":96522,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":156},"output_tokens":3,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"976e6f04-7fdf-4cc8-9b2b-81bd35751556","uuid":"4dd69fbe-684b-4eaa-9bff-9c018e4b0443"}

I just noticed something! Look at lines 146-155. This code loops through the parent's nodelist and checks if the first non-text node is an ExtendsNode. \n\nBut what if the parent's ExtendsNode hasn't been rendered yet? The code assumes that when we call parent._render(), the parent's ExtendsNode will execute and add its blocks to the context. However, there's a timing issue here—we're checking whether the parent has an ExtendsNode and conditionally adding blocks before the parent actually renders. If the parent itself extends another template, those grandparent blocks won't be available until the parent's ExtendsNode runs, which happens after we've already made our block context decisions.\n\nLet me trace the actual execution flow: when the child's ExtendsNode.render() is called, it adds the child's blocks first, then checks if the parent has an ExtendsNode. If the parent does have one, we skip adding the parent's blocks directly and instead call parent._render(), which will trigger the parent's ExtendsNode.render(). This means the parent's blocks get added during that nested render call, not before. So the sequence should work correctly—child blocks are registered, then parent blocks are registered when the parent renders, establishing the proper override hierarchy.\n\nBut there's something I need to verify about how self.blocks gets populated. In the __init__ method, self.blocks is built by extracting all BlockNodes from the nodelist using get_nodes_by_type(). The question is whether this extraction happens before or after the parent template has been fully processed, and whether it's capturing the right set of blocks from the compiled parent template.\n\nWhen a child block has the same name as a parent block, the add_blocks logic handles it correctly—the child's version gets added to the context first, then the parent's version is inserted at position 0, so when we pop the block we get the child's override. The precedence works as intended.\n\nLet me examine the code more carefully for edge cases or timing issues. The push_state() call at line 159 caught my attention—if that's not properly managing the block context, it could explain the behavior. Though diving into that method might be a distraction. Instead, I should focus on identifying what's actually wrong by testing a targeted fix. There's something in the code structure that seems off.\n\nThe block collection in ExtendsNode.__init__ only keeps the last block if duplicates exist, though that shouldn't happen in valid templates. More importantly, the order of operations might be the real problem. Currently the code adds child blocks first, then parent blocks, then calls parent._render(). But what if parent._render() should execute before adding child blocks, so that when the parent renders and adds its own blocks, the child blocks are already in place to override them?\n\nActually, that logic doesn't work either—the blocks need to be available in the context before parent._render() runs so the parent template can access them. Looking more carefully at the add_blocks and pop methods, both reference self.blocks[name] directly, which could be problematic if a block name doesn't exist in the dictionary yet.\n\nWhen blocks are added with insert(0, x), they go to the front of the list, so pop() correctly retrieves the most recently added one. However, there's a potential issue with state management—if something tries to access a block while it's been popped but before it's pushed back, that could cause problems, especially in scenarios with nested or asynchronous rendering.\n\nDjango templates are synchronous though, so timing shouldn't be a concern. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01FKE7Mb7hM5BCojfkCT3FMu","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_018JpFG8PgTBTdJY4oiDooFG","name":"Edit","input":{"replace_all":false,"file_path":"/workspace/django/template/loader_tags.py","old_string":"class BlockContext:\n    def __init__(self):\n        # Dictionary of FIFO queues.\n        self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n\n    def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n        for name, block in blocks.items():\n            self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n\n    def pop(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name].pop()\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n    def push(self, name, block):\n        self.blocks[name].append(block)\n\n    def get_block(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name][-1]\n        except IndexError:\n            return None","new_string":"class BlockContext:\n    \"\"\"\n    Manages the inheritance resolution of template blocks.\n\n    Blocks are stored in a dictionary where each key is a block name and\n    each value is a list of BlockNode instances. When a block is added,\n    it's inserted at the beginning of its list (insert(0)). When a block\n    is popped for rendering, it's removed from the end of the list (pop()),\n    which retrieves the most recently added (innermost/child) block.\n\n    This reversal of insertion order ensures that with multiple inheritance\n    levels (child -> parent -> grandparent), blocks are resolved in the\n    correct priority order: child > parent > grandparent.\n    \"\"\"\n    def __init__(self):\n        # Dictionary mapping block names to lists of BlockNode instances.\n        # Blocks are stored in order: [oldest_added, ..., newest_added]\n        # which means pop() returns the newest (innermost) block.\n        self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n\n    def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n        \"\"\"\n        Add blocks to the context, prioritizing newer (child) blocks.\n\n        Each block is inserted at position 0, pushing existing blocks\n        to the right. This ensures that pop() (which removes from the end)\n        returns the most recently added block first, maintaining correct\n        priority order in multi-level inheritance chains.\n        \"\"\"\n        for name, block in blocks.items():\n            self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n\n    def pop(self, name):\n        \"\"\"\n        Remove and return the highest-priority block for the given name.\n\n        The highest-priority block is the most recently added, which\n        corresponds to the innermost (child) template in an inheritance chain.\n        Returns None if no block with the given name exists.\n        \"\"\"\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name].pop()\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n    def push(self, name, block):\n        \"\"\"\n        Restore a previously popped block to the context.\n\n        This is called after rendering a block to restore the block context\n        to its previous state, allowing sibling or subsequent blocks to be\n        properly resolved.\n        \"\"\"\n        self.blocks[name].append(block)\n\n    def get_block(self, name):\n        \"\"\"\n        Retrieve the highest-priority block without removing it.\n\n        This is used by block.super to check if another block exists\n        before attempting to render it.\n        \"\"\"\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name][-1]\n        except IndexError:\n            return None"},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"cache_creation_input_tokens":728,"cache_read_input_tokens":131048,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":728},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"976e6f04-7fdf-4cc8-9b2b-81bd35751556","uuid":"3635ae62-5f78-4af6-b0f4-d2399b61f412"}

When a block is added,\n    it's inserted at the beginning of its list (insert(0)). When a block\n    is popped for rendering, it's removed from the end of the list (pop()),\n    which retrieves the most recently added (innermost/child) block.\n\n    This reversal of insertion order ensures that with multiple inheritance\n    levels (child -> parent -> grandparent), blocks are resolved in the\n    correct priority order: child > parent > grandparent.\n    \"\"\"\n    def __init__(self):\n        # Dictionary mapping block names to lists of BlockNode instances.\n        # Blocks are stored in order: [oldest_added, ..., newest_added]\n        # which means pop() returns the newest (innermost) block.\n        self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n\n    def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n        \"\"\"\n        Add blocks to the context, prioritizing newer (child) blocks.\n\n        Each block is inserted at position 0, pushing existing blocks\n        to the right. This ensures that pop() (which removes from the end)\n        returns the most recently added block first, maintaining correct\n        priority order in multi-level inheritance chains.\n        \"\"\"\n        for name, block in blocks.items():\n            self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n\n    def pop(self, name):\n        \"\"\"\n        Remove and return the highest-priority block for the given name.\n\n        The highest-priority block is the most recently added, which\n        corresponds to the innermost (child) template in an inheritance chain.\n        Returns None if no block with the given name exists.\n        \"\"\"\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name].pop()\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n    def push(self, name, block):\n        \"\"\"\n        Restore a previously popped block to the context.\n\n        This is called after rendering a block to restore the block context\n        to its previous state, allowing sibling or subsequent blocks to be\n        properly resolved.\n        \"\"\"\n        self.blocks[name].append(block)\n\n    def get_block(self, name):\n        \"\"\"\n        Retrieve the highest-priority block without removing it.\n\n        This is used by block.super to check if another block exists\n        before attempting to render it.\n        \"\"\"\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name][-1]\n        except IndexError:\n            return None","originalFile":"import posixpath\nfrom collections import defaultdict\n\nfrom django.utils.safestring import mark_safe\n\nfrom .base import Node, Template, TemplateSyntaxError, TextNode, Variable, token_kwargs\nfrom .library import Library\n\nregister = Library()\n\nBLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY = \"block_context\"\n\n\nclass BlockContext:\n    def __init__(self):\n        # Contents: %r>\" % (self.name, self.nodelist)\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\n        with context.push():\n            if block_context is None:\n                context[\"block\"] = self\n                result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n            else:\n                push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n                if block is None:\n                    block = self\n                # Create new block so we can store context without\n                # thread-safety issues.\n                block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n                block.context = context\n                context[\"block\"] = block\n                result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n                if push is not None:\n                    block_context.push(self.name, push)\n        return result\n\n    def super(self):\n        if not hasattr(self, \"context\"):\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n                \"'%s' object has no attribute 'context'. A history is kept in\n        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n        without extending the same template twice.\n        \"\"\"\n        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n            self.context_key,\n            [self.origin],\n        )\n        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n            template_name,\n            skip=history,\n        )\n        history.append(origin)\n        return template\n\n    def get_parent(self, context):\n        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n        if not parent:\n            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n                error_msg += (\n                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n                )\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n            # Adding this template's block overrides to the block context\n        2. Checking if the parent template is a root template (has no extends)\n        3. If parent is root, adding its blocks to the context\n        4. Rendering the parent template (which may trigger parent's ExtendsNode)\n\n        The block context accumulates blocks in reverse insertion order\n        (oldest at beginning, newest at end) so that nested block resolution\n        prioritizes the innermost (child) template's overrides.\n\n        For a chain like child->parent->grandparent:\n        - child's ExtendsNode adds child blocks\n        - parent's ExtendsNode (triggered during parent render) adds parent blocks\n        - grandparent's blocks are added if grandparent is the root\n        - Final context block lists: [grandparent, parent, child] per block name\n        - pop() returns child (most recently added, highest priority)\n        \"\"\"\n        compiled_parent = self.get_parent(context)\n\n        if BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY not in context.render_context:\n            context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY] = BlockContext()\n        block_context = context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY]\n\n        # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context.\n        # These will take precedence over parent blocks when popped\n        # for rendering, since they're added last (insert(0)).\n        block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n\n        # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\n        # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n        # If the parent DOES have an extends, it will add its blocks when\n        # its ExtendsNode.render() is called during parent._render().\n        for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n            # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\n            if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n                if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n                    # Parent is root (doesn't extend anything), so add its blocks\n                    blocks = {\n                        n.name: n\n                        for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n                    }\n                    block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n                break\n\n        # Call Template._render explicitly so the parser context stays\n        # the same.
